Name: information-theoretic_model_compression_rate-distortion
Title: Rate-Distortion Frontiers in Neural Network Compression: A Unified Framework for Pruning and Quantization
Short Hypothesis: By framing neural network compression as a rate-distortion problem, we can derive optimal pruning and quantization strategies that balance compression ratio and model performance, achieving theoretical limits of compressibility.
Related Work: Existing work on model compression often treats pruning and quantization as separate tasks, with pruning focusing on sparsity and quantization on bit-width reduction. Recent studies, such as Isik et al. (2021), have begun to explore rate-distortion theory in pruning, but a unified framework that jointly optimizes pruning and quantization remains unexplored. This proposal bridges this gap by integrating both compression techniques under a single rate-distortion framework.
Abstract: Neural network compression is essential for deploying models on resource-constrained devices. However, existing methods often treat pruning and quantization as separate tasks, leading to suboptimal compression ratios and performance degradation. This paper proposes a unified framework for neural network compression based on rate-distortion theory. By viewing compression as a lossy source coding problem, we derive optimal pruning and quantization strategies that balance the trade-off between compression ratio and model performance. Our approach provides theoretical limits on compressibility and practical algorithms that achieve these limits. We validate our framework on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet datasets, demonstrating significant improvements in compression efficiency while maintaining model accuracy.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Rate-Distortion Analysis for Pruning: We derive the rate-distortion function for pruning and compare it with existing pruning methods on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. Metrics: Compression ratio, accuracy loss.

Experiment 2: Rate-Distortion Analysis for Quantization: We derive the rate-distortion function for quantization and compare it with existing quantization methods on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. Metrics: Bit-width, accuracy loss.

Experiment 3: Unified Pruning and Quantization: We integrate pruning and quantization under a single rate-distortion framework and evaluate the combined approach on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. Metrics: Compression ratio, bit-width, accuracy loss.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Theoretical assumptions may not hold in practical settings, leading to deviations from predicted performance.
The framework may require significant computational resources to derive optimal strategies for large models.
The approach may not generalize to all types of neural architectures, particularly those with non-standard structures.
